Therefore the verdict is to keep it as a meaningful but currently unmeasurable action cluster.

coverage_assessment: The sub-actions collectively match the parent objective: they cover sediment management (Subs 1–2), dune/coastal system rehabilitation (Sub 2), engineered coastal defenses and nourishment (Sub 3), planned retreat/managed realignment tools including land acquisition (Sub 4), and protection/relocation of coastal infrastructure (Sub 5). A notable gap is the absence of explicit criteria for “high-risk areas” and outcome metrics (e.g., assets protected, shoreline length treated), which limits the ability to confirm progress against the parent’s risk-reduction goal.
coherence_assessment: The package is logically complementary: it combines nature-based measures (sediment transport, dune renaturalisation), gray infrastructure upgrades, and adaptation/retreat for areas where protection is not viable, supported by a funding line (Sub 6). There is no direct contradiction, though operational prioritization (when to defend vs retreat) is not specified.

strong_signals: Concrete intervention types (dredging, dune restoration, defense upgrades, planned retreat, land acquisition), explicit dated investment goals (2020 €47m; 2030 €560m), clear focus on high-risk coastal erosion/overtopping/flooding
initiative_reasoning: 1) The initiative aims to reduce coastal erosion/overtopping/flooding risk in high-risk coastal areas through a portfolio of nature-based actions (sediment and dunes), engineered defenses, managed retreat, and infrastructure adaptation, backed by specified public investment levels. 2) Sub-action strength count: 0 strong, 6 moderate, 0 weak—most are concrete deliverables but lack numeric outcomes and (except funding) deadlines. 3) The parent provides a clear spatial/risk scope (“areas at high risk from coastal erosion, overtopping and flooding”), but it does not establish an enforceable binding mechanism (no ordinance/mandate), so subs remain programmatic. 4) Coverage is high because the subs span the main adaptation levers (protect, accommodate, retreat) and include sediment management and habitat considerations; the main gap is measurable outcome tracking and decision criteria for where/when each intervention applies. 5) Coherence is strong because measures are complementary and can be sequenced (funding enables works; softer/nature-based and hard defenses plus retreat options), with no contradictions. 6) Applying the initiative soundness rule: it cannot be SOUND because aggregate_measurability is 'No' and there is no 'strong' sub-action (no explicit measurable outcomes with deadlines). initiative_name: Annex I -Lines of action and measures / Line of Action #9 - Development of decision support tools, capacity building and awareness-raising actions
parent_statement: Line of Action #9: Develop decision support tools, capacity building and awareness-raising actions, including developing a National Adaptation Platform, producing climate risk mapping, developing adaptation plans, and setting up forecasting/warning/response systems.
coverage_assessment: The sub-actions collectively match the parent’s stated scope: decision-support tools (platform/platform data), capacity building, climate impact monitoring and risk mapping, adaptation planning, forecasting/warning/response systems, communications to health authorities, and M&E arrangements across levels of government. The main gap is the absence of explicit performance targets (e.g., coverage of mapped areas, number of plans produced) and timelines for delivery.
coherence_assessment: The sub-actions are complementary and logically sequenced: information systems and mapping (Actions 1 and 3) support planning (Action 4) and operational warning/response (Actions 4 and 5), with capacity building (Action 2) enabling use, and M&E (Action 6) closing the loop. There is minor overlap between Actions 1 and 3 (both address information systems), but they are not contradictory.
weak_signals: No explicit deadlines/years, no quantified outcome targets, capacity building framed without scope/metrics
strong_signals: National Adaptation Platform, climate risk mapping, forecasting/warning/response systems, climate prediction models for extreme phenomena, monitoring and evaluation arrangements across governance levels
initiative_reasoning: 1) The initiative aims to create the institutional and technical backbone for climate adaptation decision-making: platforms and tools, climate impact monitoring and risk mapping, adaptation plans, early warning/response systems, communications, and M&E. 2) Sub-action strengths: 0 strong, 5 moderate (Actions 1, 3, 4, 5, 6), 1 weak (Action 2) due to being a broad capacity-building statement without metrics/timeline. 3) The parent provides broad scope (national/regional/inter-municipal/municipal implied by Action 6 and the line-of-action framing), but it does not provide an enforceable binding mechanism (no ordinance/mandate) and does not add timelines/targets that would lift measurability. 4) Coverage is high (0.85) because the sub-actions directly correspond to each component named in the parent; coherence is strong (0.80) because actions form a sensible delivery chain from data/tools to planning and warning to communications and evaluation, with only minor overlap between information-system items. 5) Applying the soundness rule: it is not SOUND because aggregate_measurability is 'No' and there are no 'strong' sub-actions; it is also not purely aspirational because multiple concrete systems and plans are to be developed, so it qualifies as PARTIAL. Verdict: keep as an actionable enabling initiative, but it needs deadlines and measurable indicators to become trackable.
